annotation.message: Any fire in an enclosed space requires adequate ventilation to prevent carbon monoxide (CO) poisoning. CO is odorless and colorless -- symptoms include headache, dizziness, and nausea, progressing to unconsciousness and death. Ensure cross-ventilation with an inlet and outlet. Never sleep with a fire burning in an unvented space.
annotation.source: CDC Carbon Monoxide Poisoning Prevention; National Fire Protection Association

annotation.message: For outdoor fires: clear a 10-foot radius of flammable material, build a rock or dirt ring for containment, always have water or dirt ready to extinguish, never leave unattended, and fully extinguish before leaving -- stir ashes and add water until no steam or smoke remains.
annotation.source: US Forest Service Leave No Trace Guidelines; National Fire Protection Association

annotation.message: Forge, kiln, and high-temperature fire work requires protective equipment: leather aprons, heat-resistant gloves, eye protection (face shield for forge work), and leather or wool clothing (never synthetic fabrics near open flame). Ensure a clear path to exit the work area and keep a fire extinguisher rated for all fire types nearby.
annotation.source: Foxfire Book 8 (1984): Blacksmithing; NFPA Forge Safety Guidelines

annotation.message: Using flammable accelerants (gasoline, kerosene, lighter fluid) to start or intensify fires is extremely dangerous and falls outside traditional heritage fire practices. Traditional fire-starting methods (friction fire, flint and steel, char cloth) are safer and more appropriate to heritage skills instruction. Please use traditional fire-starting techniques.
annotation.source: National Fire Protection Association; Heritage Skills Scope Definition

annotation.message: The qulliq (Inuit oil lamp) is a sacred and carefully managed flame. When using a qulliq or any open oil lamp in an enclosed space, ensure adequate ventilation to prevent CO buildup and smoke accumulation. Traditional Inuit snow shelters (igluit) maintain ventilation holes -- these are essential, not optional.
annotation.source: Inuit Tapiriit Kanatami Educational Resources; Pitseolak Ashoona documentation
